topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope lay on the kitchen counter, stark white against the worn wood. Liam picked it up, his fingers tracing the embossed address with a slow, deliberate motion. Across the line, the words swam: 'New York, New York.' A cold knot tightened in his chest, a physical pressure that made his breath catch. He reread the note three times, the ink blurring slightly under his intense gaze. He didn't want to believe it. He pushed the paper away, the sound a sharp rip in the quiet morning. He needed to look at the other side of the room, anything but the evidence. He started scrubbing the already clean counter with unnecessary vigor, the rhythmic scraping a frantic attempt to dislodge the sudden, sickening shift in his equilibrium.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" Maya managed, the word coming out as a thin, reedy sound. She stared at the moving boxes stacked haphazardly in the hallway, each one a monument to departure. The reality of the situation—the sheer distance, the abruptness of the decision—felt like a physical blow to her sternum. She clenched her jaw so tightly her molars ached. She walked over to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to glimpse the familiar street, trying to anchor herself to the familiar. But the sight only amplified the emptiness where her best friend used to be. She turned back, her expression carefully blank, forcing a smile that felt brittle on her face. "That’s just... a big change," she murmured, the tone carefully neutral, though the tremor in her hands betrayed the internal upheaval.

[story 3]

He paced the length of the living room, his footsteps echoing loudly on the hardwood floor. Every time he stopped, he ran a hand through his hair, feeling the tension coil tighter in his neck muscles. The information had landed like a stone in his gut, heavy and immovable. He picked up his phone, his thumb hovering over the contact, then letting it drop onto the sofa cushion with a dull thud. He didn't call. He just sat there, staring at the ceiling, willing the feeling to dissipate. He forced himself to focus on the mundane—the pattern of the wallpaper, the dust motes dancing in the afternoon light—anything to keep the churning sensation inside from overwhelming him. He didn't move toward the door; he simply stayed put, rooted to the spot.
